content:
5 min read
4 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I wanted to build something that behaved like a production system; something that could wake up, collect data, generate content, publish a website, and go back to sleep without me touching it. Football felt like the perfect domain to try that with.
This is the story of how I built the World Cup Intelligence Hub, what broke, what I learned, and why I’d do it all again.
Interact with the full project on my GITHUB
The Idea
The pitch I gave myself was simple: build a system that runs a miniature content operation, end-to-end, with minimal human involvement.
No manually updating pages. No copying scores from one place to another. The platform would:

• Pull live tournament data from an API (football-data-org)

• Cache it locally for reliability

• Generate AI-powered readable content from that data

• Build and deploy a static website

• Do all of this on a schedule, automatically
Think of it less as a football website and more as a self-operating publishing pipeline, football just happened to be the content.
How the Data Layer Works
The foundation of everything is reliable data.
I used the Football Data API as my source and built collectors that pull fixtures, results, match information, and tournament stages. The collectors don’t just dump raw JSON, they normalize each response into a consistent internal structure so the rest of the platform doesn’t have to care about how the API formats things.
That normalization step sounds minor. It isn’t. It’s what lets you swap data sources later without rewriting your content generation logic.
The Caching Layer (The Lesson I’m Glad I Learned Early)
Here’s something you find out quickly when building on third-party APIs: they fail. Rate limits, outages, network will definitely happen.
Early in the build, I made the platform brittle by design; no API response, no website update. That wasn’t acceptable.
So I implemented a local caching layer. Every successful API response gets saved. If the API is unavailable on the next run, the platform falls back to cached data automatically. The website keeps working. The scheduler keeps running. Nothing breaks visibly.
It sounds simple in hindsight. But building that fallback changes how you think about the entire system, you stop assuming the happy path and start designing for failure modes.
Content Generation
Once the data pipeline was stable, the interesting part began: turning structured data into actual readable content.
The platform generates two types:
Match reports — post-match articles built from completed fixture data. Not just “Team A beat Team B 2–1.” The goal was prose a fan could read.
Match previews — pre-match articles for upcoming games, generated before kick-off.
Automating this forced me to think carefully about what makes a match article useful. It’s not just the data but the framing as well. Getting the generation logic right took more iteration than I expected.
Building the Website
The static site has a few main sections: Home, Fixtures, Reports, and Host Cities.
The Host Cities section became the most interesting part of the project. What started as a simple list of venues evolved into an interactive explorer where visitors can:

• Search nearby attractions and restaurants

• Find fan zones and stadiums

• Use geolocation to discover what’s around them
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That scope expansion turned a football scores site into something closer to a travel companion for World Cup attendees. I hadn’t planned it that way, it emerged from thinking about who would actually use the site.
Interact with the website HERE

Automation with GitHub Actions
The automation layer is what makes this feel like a real system and not just a script you run manually.
GitHub Actions handles everything on a schedule:

• Trigger the data collection scripts

• Generate updated content

• Build the static site

• Deploy to GitHub Pages
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The platform runs whether I’m with my laptop or not. That was the original goal, and reaching it felt significant, not because it’s technically complex, but because it changes the mental model of what you’ve built. It’s no longer a project but an actual system.
The Problems (Because There Were Several)
No production-style system gets built cleanly. Here’s what actually went wrong:
Missing reports on the live site — Generated articles weren’t appearing after deployment. The cause was a version control conflict: .gitignore rules were excluding the generated files from commits. The files existed locally, never made it to the repo, never deployed. Once I understood the pattern, it was a straightforward fix, but tracing it through multiple layers of the pipeline took time.
Host Cities page working locally, broken on GitHub Pages — A classic static site trap. The page rendered fine in local development but failed after deployment because of how the JavaScript was referencing assets. Path handling that works against a local dev server doesn’t always work against a CDN. I had to rethink how resources were being loaded for the deployed environment specifically.
Match scores not displaying — The scores existed in the API response. They just never made it to the rendered page. The issue was in the rendering layer; the data was being collected and stored, but never passed into the template. Once I found where the chain broke, the fix was small. Finding it wasn’t.
These bugs are worth mentioning because they’re the kind that test whether your debugging instincts are actually good. When something fails across multiple layers API, data store, generator, template, deployment. you can’t just guess. You have to trace systematically.
Conclusion
A few things became genuinely clearer after building this end-to-end:
Fallback mechanisms aren’t optional. Any pipeline that touches external services needs to assume those services will fail. Design for that from the start.
Static sites are more powerful than they get credit for. When you combine pre-generated content with a solid build pipeline, you get something fast, cheap to host, and surprisingly capable.
GitHub Actions is an underrated production tool. For a project like this, it functions as a lightweight job scheduler, CI system, and deployment pipeline all in one. For personal projects and portfolio work, it removes the need for a separate server entirely.
Automation surfaces your edge cases. When a person is clicking through a workflow, they naturally handle exceptions. Automation doesn’t. Every assumption you make about your data or your pipeline will eventually be tested by something unexpected.
I initially planned x and medium posts automations but both involve some paid tier on APIs so I will be adding those features when I have enough money to spare on personal projects.
Thank you for staying till this point, see you again in my next project
